narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and personal factors from past students. The model estimated an 82.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes and resources. This decision hinged primarily on your academic performance in school, particularly your grades in later years. The strongest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1st, meaning you placed in the bottom 10% of your school that year—far below the typical student's 5th or 6th decile average—which signaled to the model a significant drop-off in performance when coursework gets tougher. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2nd, again in the bottom 20% compared to the average around the 6th decile, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic struggles from the start. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, slightly below the average of 3.21, actually worked a bit in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, showing some resilience in college. Other elements like your LSAT score of 27—well under the typical 37—and your plan to attend full-time, which is common for 93% of students, played lesser roles but didn't offset the key academic red flags. Compared to typical admitted students, who often rank in the middle or top of their classes throughout, your lower deciles stood out as the biggest departure, making the model view your bar success as riskier. While factors like your family income bracket aligned with many applicants, they couldn't counterbalance the academic indicators. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven call based on patterns from thousands of similar profiles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and reapplying after building a stronger academic narrative—many students in your position have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps. Schools value persistence, and targeted improvements here could flip the model's outlook next time.
